subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor is filled equally (half) with two dielectrics of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, as shown in figures. The distance between the plates is d and area of each plate is A. If capacitance in first configuration and second configuration are $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively, then $\frac{C_1}{C_2}$ is :
question: A parallel plate capacitor is filled equally (half) with two dielectrics of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, as shown in figures. The distance between the plates is d and area of each plate is A. If capacitance in first configuration and second configuration are $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively, then $\frac{C_1}{C_2}$ is :
solution:
Area of plate is $A$. then
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}} \\ & \mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}
\end{aligned}$
Let $\mathrm{C}_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}=2 \varepsilon_2 \mathrm{C}_0 \\ & \mathrm{C}^{\prime}=2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{C} \& \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ are in series
$\mathrm{C}_1=\frac{\mathrm{CC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{C}^{\prime}}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_0\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)}$
$=\frac{2 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0}{\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)}$
Here $\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \mathrm{~d}}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0}{2}$
$\mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon_2 \mathrm{C}_0}{2}$
$\mathrm{C} \& \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ are inparallel
$\mathrm{C}_2=\mathrm{C}^{\prime}+\mathrm{C}=\left(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2\right) \frac{\mathrm{C}_0}{2}$
Thus $\frac{\mathrm{C}_1}{\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0}{\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)} \times \frac{2}{\left(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2\right) \mathrm{C}_0}$
$=\frac{4 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1}{\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)^2}$

options: Work done by the battery is half of the energy dissipated in the resistor
solution:
q = CV 1 - e t / τ
 
At t = 2 τ

q = CV 1 - e - 2

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> wire is bend to form a square loop. A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery with internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across one of its sides. If a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is connected across one of its diagonals, the energy stored by the capacitor will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>μJ</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> wire is bend to form a square loop. A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery with internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across one of its sides. If a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is connected across one of its diagonals, the energy stored by the capacitor will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>μJ</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
Under the balanced state, there is no flow of charge through the capacitor. So, the path connecting the capacitor behaves as an open path.

The equivalent resistance of the entire circuit, under equilibrium condition, can be calculated as follows:

Req=1+114+4+4+14 Ω=1+3 Ω=4 Ω

Hence, the current through the entire circuit is given by

I=VReq=94 A

So, the current I1 can be written as

I1=94×416 A=916 A

The potential difference between the points A and B is,

VA-VB=I1×8=916×8=92 V

Hence, the energy stored in the capacitor is given by

U=12CVA-VB2=12×4×814 μJ=812 μJ

∴x=81

subject: Physics
q:
The displacement current of 4.425 μA is developed in the space between the plates of parallel plate capacitor when voltage is changing at a rate of 106 V s-1. The area of each plate of the capacitor is 40 cm2. The distance between each plate of the capacitor is x×10-3 m. The value of x is ,
(Permittivity of free space, ε0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2) _______
question:
The displacement current of 4.425 μA is developed in the space between the plates of parallel plate capacitor when voltage is changing at a rate of 106 V s-1. The area of each plate of the capacitor is 40 cm2. The distance between each plate of the capacitor is x×10-3 m. The value of x is ,
(Permittivity of free space, ε0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2) _______
solution:
Displacement current is given by id=ε0dϕEdt

Or id=ε0ddtEA, where E=qAε0

Or id=ε0ddtqAAε0=dqdt=ddtCV

Or id=CdVdt=ε0AddVdt

Putting the values, we have 

4.425×10-6=8.85×10-12×40×10-4×106 d

d=2×10-6×10-4×106×40

d=80×10-4=8×10-3 m

Hence, value of x=8.

subject: Physics
q: Consider a circuit consisting of a capacitor ($20\ \mu$F), resistor ($100\ \Omega$) and two identical diodes as shown in figure. The resistance of diode under forward biasing condition is $10\ \Omega$. The time constant of the circuit is $\alpha \times 10^{-3}$ s. The value of $\alpha$ is _____
question: Consider a circuit consisting of a capacitor ($20\ \mu$F), resistor ($100\ \Omega$) and two identical diodes as shown in figure. The resistance of diode under forward biasing condition is $10\ \Omega$. The time constant of the circuit is $\alpha \times 10^{-3}$ s. The value of $\alpha$ is _____
solution:
From the given circuit diagram, the battery is connected such that its positive terminal is upwards. This means the current will flow in the clockwise direction during the charging of the capacitor.

In the parallel combination of the two diodes, the left diode is pointing downwards (in the direction of the current) and will be forward-biased. The right diode is pointing upwards (opposite to the current) and will be reverse-biased.

The reverse-biased diode offers infinite resistance and acts as an open circuit. The forward-biased diode offers a resistance of $10\ \Omega$.

The total equivalent resistance of the circuit is the sum of the resistor $R$ and the resistance of the forward-biased diode:
$R_{eq} = 100\ \Omega + 10\ \Omega = 110\ \Omega$

The time constant $\tau$ of the $RC$ circuit is given by:
$\tau = R_{eq}C$
$\tau = 110 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$
$\tau = 2200 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 2.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$

Comparing this with the given time constant $\alpha \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$, we get:
$\alpha = 2.2$

Answer: $2.2$

A parallel plate capacitor is formed by two plates each of area 30πcm2 separated by 1 mm. A material of dielectric strength 3.6×107 V m-1 is filled between the plates. If the maximum charge that can be stored on the capacitor without causing any dielectric breakdown is 7×10-6 C, the value of dielectric constant of the material is :

[Use 14πεo=9×109 N m2 C-2]
question:
A parallel plate capacitor is formed by two plates each of area 30πcm2 separated by 1 mm. A material of dielectric strength 3.6×107 V m-1 is filled between the plates. If the maximum charge that can be stored on the capacitor without causing any dielectric breakdown is 7×10-6 C, the value of dielectric constant of the material is :

[Use 14πεo=9×109 N m2 C-2]
solution:
The dielectric strength given is 3.6×107 V m-1.and the separation is 1 mm. Therefore, maximum potential difference that can be applied will be,

Vmax=3.6×107×d.

Now,

C=qmaxVmax⇒kε0Ad=7×10-73.6×107×dk=dε0×30π×10-4×7×10-73.6×107×d=2.33

question: A parallel plate capacitor has plates of area A separated by distance d between them. It is filled with a dielectric which has a dielectric constant that varies as Kx=K01+αx where x is the distance measured from one of the plates. If αd<<1, the total capacitance of the system is best given by the expression:
solution:
Capacitance of element, dC=K01+αxε0Adx
∫1dC=∫0ddxK0ε0A1+αx
⇒1C=1K0ε0Aαln1+αd
Given αd≪1
⇒1C=1K0ε0Aααd-α2d22
⇒1C=dK0ε0A1-αd2
⇒C=K0ε0Ad1+αd2

subject: Physics
q:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Effective capacitance of a series combination of capacitors is always smaller than the smallest capacitance of the capacitor in the combination.
B. When a dielectric medium is placed between the charged plates of a capacitor, displacement of charges cannot occur due to insulation property of dielectric.
C. Increasing of area of capacitor plate or decreasing of thickness of dielectric is an alternate method to increase the capacitance.
D. For a point charge, concentric spherical shells centered at the location of the charge are equipotential surfaces.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Effective capacitance of a series combination of capacitors is always smaller than the smallest capacitance of the capacitor in the combination.
B. When a dielectric medium is placed between the charged plates of a capacitor, displacement of charges cannot occur due to insulation property of dielectric.
C. Increasing of area of capacitor plate or decreasing of thickness of dielectric is an alternate method to increase the capacitance.
D. For a point charge, concentric spherical shells centered at the location of the charge are equipotential surfaces.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
A. Effective capacitance of series combination is always smaller than the smallest capacitance - TRUE. For series: $\frac{1}{C_{eff}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \ldots$, therefore $C_{eff} < C_{min}$
B. Dielectric prevents charge displacement - FALSE. A dielectric allows polarization (displacement of bound charges) but prevents free charge movement. It does not prevent displacement of bound charges.
C. Increasing area or decreasing thickness increases capacitance - TRUE. From $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$, increasing $A$ or decreasing $d$ increases $C$.
D. Concentric spherical shells centered at a point charge location are equipotential surfaces - TRUE. For a point charge, the potential at distance $r$ is $V = \frac{kq}{r}$, which depends only on distance, making concentric spheres equipotential surfaces.
Correct statements: A, C, D

q: A time varying potential difference is applied between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $2.5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$. The dielectric constant of the medium between the capacitor plates is 1 . It produces an instantaneous displacement current of 0.25 mA in the intervening space between the capacitor plates, the magnitude of the rate of change of the potential difference will be _____ $\mathrm{Vs}^{-1}$.
question: A time varying potential difference is applied between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $2.5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$. The dielectric constant of the medium between the capacitor plates is 1 . It produces an instantaneous displacement current of 0.25 mA in the intervening space between the capacitor plates, the magnitude of the rate of change of the potential difference will be _____ $\mathrm{Vs}^{-1}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{CdV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{d}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{d}}}{\mathrm{C}} \\ & =\frac{0.25 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & =100\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In the given figure the total charge stored in the combination of capacitors is 100 µC. The value of ‘x’ is ______________.
question: In the given figure the total charge stored in the combination of capacitors is 100 µC. The value of ‘x’ is ______________.
solution:
All the capacitors are in parallel combination and therefore potential drop across each capacitor would be the same.

Charge on the capacitor can be written as, Q=CV.

The charge on C1 is Q1=2×10=20 μC

The charge on C2 is Q2=x×10=10x μC

The charge on C3 is Q3=3×10=30 μC

By the given data.

C1+C2+C3=100 μC

⇒102+x+3=100

⇒x=5

q: Two capacitors of capacitances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> are charged to potential differences <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>V</mi></math>, respectively. These are then connected in parallel in such a manner that the positive terminal of one is connected to the negative terminal of the other. The final energy of this configuration is :
question: Two capacitors of capacitances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> are charged to potential differences <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>V</mi></math>, respectively. These are then connected in parallel in such a manner that the positive terminal of one is connected to the negative terminal of the other. The final energy of this configuration is :
solution:
Charge on C=CV
Charge on 2C=2C2V

From charge conservation, 2C(2V)-CV=(C+2C)V'
Common potential, V'=V
Final energy, Uf=12CV2+12×2CV2=32CV2

q: A condenser of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitance is charged steadily from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following graph represents correctly the variation of potential difference<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> across its plates with respect to the charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Q</mi></mfenced></math> on the condenser?
question: A condenser of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitance is charged steadily from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following graph represents correctly the variation of potential difference<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> across its plates with respect to the charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Q</mi></mfenced></math> on the condenser?
solution:
The charge on a condenser is given by Q=CV.

⇒V=1CQ

Since the capacitance depends on the construction of the condenser, it is constant. Therefore, the graph will be straight line with slope =1C.
Slope =1C=12×10-6=5×105

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor with plate separation 5 mm is charged by a battery. On introducing a mica sheet of 2 mm and maintaining the connections of the plates with the terminals of the battery, it is found that it draws $25 \%$ more charge from the battery. The dielectric constant of mica is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A parallel plate capacitor with plate separation 5 mm is charged by a battery. On introducing a mica sheet of 2 mm and maintaining the connections of the plates with the terminals of the battery, it is found that it draws $25 \%$ more charge from the battery. The dielectric constant of mica is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Initial capacitor has plate separation 5 mm with capacitance $C_1 = \epsilon_0 A / 5$.
After inserting 2 mm mica sheet, the gap remaining is 3 mm of air. The effective separation becomes: $d_{eff} = 3 + 2/k$ where $k$ is the dielectric constant of mica.
New capacitance: $C_2 = \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{3 + 2/k}$
Since battery remains connected, voltage is constant. The charge is: $Q = CV$
Ratio of charges: $\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{\epsilon_0 A/(3 + 2/k)}{\epsilon_0 A/5} = \frac{5}{3 + 2/k}$
Given that charge increases by 25%: $Q_2 = 1.25 Q_1$
$\frac{5}{3 + 2/k} = 1.25$
$5 = 1.25(3 + 2/k)$
$5 = 3.75 + 2.5/k$
$1.25 = 2.5/k$
$k = 2$

subject: Physics
q:
A capacitor of $10 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitance whose plates are separated by $10 \mathrm{~mm}$ through air and each plate has area $4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ is now filled equally with two dielectric media of $\mathrm{K}_1=2, \mathrm{~K}_2=3$ respectively as shown in figure. If new force between the plates is $8 \mathrm{~N}$. The supply voltage is _________ $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~V}$.
(we modified language of question to make it correct)
question:
A capacitor of $10 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitance whose plates are separated by $10 \mathrm{~mm}$ through air and each plate has area $4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ is now filled equally with two dielectric media of $\mathrm{K}_1=2, \mathrm{~K}_2=3$ respectively as shown in figure. If new force between the plates is $8 \mathrm{~N}$. The supply voltage is _________ $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~V}$.
(we modified language of question to make it correct)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2 \\ & \mathrm{C}_1=\frac{2 \epsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \times \mathrm{d}}=10 \mu \mathrm{F} \\ & \mathrm{C}_2=\frac{3 \epsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \mathrm{~d}}=15 \mu \mathrm{F} \\ & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=25 \mu \mathrm{F} \end{aligned}$
Now the charge on $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_1=10 \mathrm{~V} \mu \mathrm{c} \\ & \mathrm{C}_2=1.5 \mathrm{~V} \mu \mathrm{C} . \end{aligned}$
Now force between the plates $\left[F=\frac{Q^2}{2 A \in_0}\right]$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{100 \mathrm{~V}^2 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \epsilon_0}+\frac{225 \mathrm{~V}^2 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times \epsilon_0}=8 \\ & 325 \mathrm{~V}^2=8 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.85 \\ & \mathrm{~V}^2=\frac{32 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-4}}{325} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{V}=\sqrt{\frac{283.2 \times 10^{-4}}{325}} \\ & \mathrm{~V}=0.93 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}$

q: A capacitor with capacitance <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is charged to <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . If the plates are pulled apart to reduce the capacitance to <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>, how much work is done?
question: A capacitor with capacitance <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is charged to <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . If the plates are pulled apart to reduce the capacitance to <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>, how much work is done?
solution:
Uinitial=Q22C1
Ufinal=Q22C2
Work done =Ufinal-Uinitial
=Q22C2-Q22C1
=5×10-622×2×10-6-5×10-622×5×10-6 
=254-52×10-6
=154×10-6 =3.75×10-6 J

The charging battery is now disconnected and a porcelain slab of dielectric constant <math><mn>6.5</mn></math> is slipped between the plates. The work done by the capacitor on the slab is
question: A parallel plate capacitor having capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>pF</mi></math> is charged by a battery to a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> between its plates. The charging battery is now disconnected and a porcelain slab of dielectric constant <math><mn>6.5</mn></math> is slipped between the plates. The work done by the capacitor on the slab is
solution:
Initial energy of capacitor

Ui=12q2c
=12×120×12012=600 pJ

Since battery is disconnected so charge remain same. Final energy of capacitor

 Uf=12q2c k

=12×120×12012×6.5=92 pJ

W+Uf=Ui 
W=508 pJ

q: The equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$ in the circuit given below is:
question: The equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$ in the circuit given below is:
solution:
The simplified circuit of the circuit given in question as follows:
The equivalent capacitance between C \& D capacitors of $2 \mu \mathrm{F}, 5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ are in parallel. $\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{CD}}=2+5+5=12 \mu \mathrm{F}(\because$ In parallel grouping $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2+\ldots .+\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}$ ) Similarly equivalent capacitance between $\mathrm{E}$ $\& \mathrm{BC}_{\mathrm{EB}}$ $=4+2=6 \mu \mathrm{F}$ Now equivalent capacitance between A \& B $\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{5}{12}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{12}{5}=2.4 \mu \mathrm{F}(\because$ In series grouping, $\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_2}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}}$ )

subject: Physics
q: A capacitance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi>μF</mi></math> is required in an electrical circuit across a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math>. A large number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitors are available which can withstand a potential difference of not more than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mn>0</mn><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The minimum number of capacitors required to achieve this is:
question: A capacitance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi>μF</mi></math> is required in an electrical circuit across a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math>. A large number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitors are available which can withstand a potential difference of not more than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mn>0</mn><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The minimum number of capacitors required to achieve this is:
solution:
Let us assume that we connect n(whole number) capacitors in series such that the potential difference across the combination is 1000 V. This means the potential difference across each capacitor is

Vcapacitor=1000n V≤300 V

n⩾1000300=3.333.

nmin=4

This means we need to connect at least 4 capacitors in series to make sure that the potential across each one is less than 300 V.

Ceq=mnC=2 μF

m4=2⇒m=8

∴ Minimum no. of capacitors =8×4=32

q: Two capacitors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are charged to <math> <mrow> <mtext>120 V</mtext> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <mtext>200 V</mtext> </mrow> </math> respectively. It is found that by connecting them together the potential on each one can be made zero. Then:
question: Two capacitors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are charged to <math> <mrow> <mtext>120 V</mtext> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <mtext>200 V</mtext> </mrow> </math> respectively. It is found that by connecting them together the potential on each one can be made zero. Then:
solution:
For potential to be made zero, after connection

120C1=200C2

⇒3C1=5C2

subject: Physics
q: A resistor 'R' and $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor in series is connected through a switch to $200 \mathrm{~V}$ direct supply. Across the capacitor is a neon bulb that lights up at $120 \mathrm{~V}$. Calculate the value of $R$ to make the bulb light up $5 \mathrm{~s}$ after the switch has been closed. $\left(\log _{10} 2.5=0.4\right)$
question: A resistor 'R' and $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor in series is connected through a switch to $200 \mathrm{~V}$ direct supply. Across the capacitor is a neon bulb that lights up at $120 \mathrm{~V}$. Calculate the value of $R$ to make the bulb light up $5 \mathrm{~s}$ after the switch has been closed. $\left(\log _{10} 2.5=0.4\right)$
solution:
$V_c=E\left(1-e^{-t / R c}\right)$
$1-e^{-t / R c}=\frac{120}{200}=\frac{3}{5}$
$\Rightarrow R=\frac{5}{1.84 \times 10^{-6}}=2.7 \times 10^6 \Omega$

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi></math> capacitor is charged by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected to another uncharged <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi></math> capacitor. Electrostatic energy lost in the process is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μJ</mi></math>.
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi></math> capacitor is charged by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected to another uncharged <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi></math> capacitor. Electrostatic energy lost in the process is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μJ</mi></math>.
solution:
The formula to calculate the initial energy stored in the first capacitor is given by

Ui=12CV2   ...1

The formula to calculate the final energy stored when two capacitors are connected is given by 

Uf=12CV22×2=14CV2   ...2

Subtract equation (2) from equation (1) to obtain the energy loss ∆U.

 ∆U=12CV2-14CV2= CV24   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required loss.

∆U=600×10-12×(200)24 J= 6 μJ

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is charged fully by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected in series to another uncharged <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor. If the energy change during the charge redistribution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is :
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is charged fully by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected in series to another uncharged <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor. If the energy change during the charge redistribution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is :
solution:
C1=5μF  V1=220Volt

C2=2.5μF  V2=0

Heat loss;

ΔH=U1-Ut=12C1C2C1+C2v1-v22

=12×5×2.5(5+2.5)(220-0)2μJ

=52×3×22×22×100×10-6J

=5×11×223×10-4J=55×223×10-4J

=12103×10-4J=12103×10-3J=4×10-2

According to questions

x100=4×10-2

so, x=4

subject: Physics
q: The distance between two plates of a capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and its capacitance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when air is the medium between the plates. If a metal sheet of thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>d</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> and of the same area as plate is introduced between the plates, the capacitance of the capacitor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> . The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: The distance between two plates of a capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and its capacitance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when air is the medium between the plates. If a metal sheet of thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>d</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> and of the same area as plate is introduced between the plates, the capacitance of the capacitor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> . The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
The formula of capacitance is C1=ε0Ad

The dielectric constant of metal sheet is Kmetal=∞

The new value of capacitance is 

C2=ε0Ad-t+tKmetal=ε0Ad-2d3+0=3C1

Clearly, the ratio,

C2C1=3C1C1=3:1
